content.full_text:
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
(916) 322·5662 
Mi chae 1 Dor n 
Director of Planning 
City of Gilroy 
P. O. Box 66 
Gilroy, CA 
95020 
De arM r. Do r n : 
Administration 
322-5660 
Executive/legal 
322·5901 
April 13, 1983 
Enforcement 
322~1 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
Re: 
Your Request for Written 
Advice, 
Our No. A-83-086 
You have written to request our aaVlce regarding possible 
conflicts of interest arising from your prospective purchase of 
a lot in Gilroy on which to build a home. 
Your letter states 
the facts and questions as follows: 
FACTS 
You are the Director of Planning for the City of Gilroy 
having authority for recommendations to both the Planning 
Commission and City Council on virtually all land development 
proposals. 
Due to the small size of the planning staff, it 
would be impractical for you to remove yourself from the review 
of development applications. 
You have been looking for a place 
to live in Gilroy for more than a year. 
However, you were 
unable to find a satisfactory home and so decided you should 
have a home built. 
About six months ago a few lots which meet 
your needs were offered for sale in a new tract. 
You waited 
until all planning approvals were completed on this tract, 
before making an offer, through a real estate agent, on a one 
acre lot, March 17th. 
The asking price for the lot is $90,000; 
your first offer was $71,800 with the present owner to carry a 
first morgage of $50,000 for five years. 
You are awaiting a 
counter offer. 
On March 21st, you learned that the present 
partners who own the lot, in addition to a local consulting 
engineer, include a family group which is extensively involved 
in land development in Gilroy. 
The Planning Department is 
presently processing three other requests by this family for 
development of several hundred acres in various areas of the 
Ci ty. 


Michael Dorn 
April 13, 1983 
Page 2 
QUESTIONS 
1. 
Should you still pursue purchase of this loti and, if 
so, what should you do to avoid any possible conflict 
of interest? 
2. 
Due to the small community, you have professional 
dealings with virtually every local architect and 
contractor. 
What additional problems could this 
situation pose for you in having your proposed home 
designed and built? 
ANALYSIS 
Under the terms of your offer, the owners of the lot would 
carry back a first mortgage of $50,000. 
This would constitute a 
loan to you of that sum for the period of five years. 
Under the 
Political Reform Act!/ an official may not make or participate 
in making or use his or her official position to influence any 
decision in which he or she has a financial interest. 
Section 
87100. 
What constitutes a financial interest is defined by 
Section 87103 as follows: 
An official has a financial interest in a decision 
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on 
the public generally, on: 
(c) 
Any source of income, other than loans by a 
commercial lending institution in the regular course of 
business on terms available to the public without 
regard to official status, aggregating two hundred 
fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, 
received by or promised to the public official within 
12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. 
"Income" is defined to include any loan of money in Section 
82030(a); there are certain limited exceptions under Section 
82030(b) which do not apply here. 
Since the owners will be 
!/ Government Code Sections 81000-91014; all statutory 
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise 
specified. 


Michael Dorn 
April 13, 1983 
Page 3 
loaning you $50,000, this will make them a source of income to 
you of $250 or more from the time that your offer (or any 
similar offer) is accepted up until the time that the loan 
balance falls below $250. 
For that time period and for 12 
months thereafter, you will be prohibited from making, 
participating in, or influencing decisions which would have a 
"material financial effect distinguishable from the effect on 
the public generally" as to any of the individuals or 
partnerships involved in the loan. 
It is likely that many 
decisions of the type in which you are involved in your capacity 
as Director of Planning will have such an impact on the 
individuals involved. 
What consitutes a material financial 
effect within the meaning of section 87103 is defined in our 
regulation, 2 Cal. Adm. Code section 18702 (copy enclosed) . 
To avoid any possibility of a conflict of interest arising 
out of your purchase of the property, you should secure 
independent financing from a commercial lending institution on 
terms available to the general public or from relatives or other 
sources outside your jurisdiction (Gilroy) and thereby pay cash 
to the owners of the lot. 
Your second question is more general in nature. 
However, we 
will try to provide you with some guidance. 
At the point in 
time where you have a specific question, please provide us with 
the opportunity to render specific advice. 
Generally, a contractor or an architect will receive income 
from you and not become a source of income to you. 
Consequently, your private relationships with these individuals 
would not give rise to a need for disqualification in your 
professional relationships with them.~ Obviously, you will 
have to disqualify yourself from any role in Planning Department 
or City decisions affecting your own property. 
~/ However, if an architect or a contractor provided a 
rebate or discount to you because of your official status, then 
disqualification would be required for the following reasons: 
"Income" under Section 82030(a) also includes any "gift." 
"Gift" is defined under Section 82028 to include any rebate or 
discount "unless the rebate or discount is made in the regular 
course of business to members of tne public without regard to 
official status." 
Receipt of a "gift" would require 
disqualification for 12 months as to any decisions such as those 
discussed above. 


Michael Dorn 
April 13, 1983 
Page 4 
Should you have future questions or questions about this 
letter, please do not hesitate to contact me at (916) 322-5901. 
REL:plh 
Enclosure 
Sincerely, 
~/-f<-~ 
f.e{,'~,-,-: (- 7-~ 
Robert E. teidigh 
Counsel 
Legal Division 


~ift! of ~iIr0t! 
10 00 AH 'iY}Sl Rosanna Street, P. O. Box 66 
GILROY, CALIFORNIA 
95020 
Bob Leidigh 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
P.O. Box 807 
Sacramento, CA 95804 
Dear Mr. Leidigh: 
March 23, 1983 
Telephone 842-2137 
PLANNING DEPARTMENT 
I am writing in order to obtain a written confirmation of our telephone 
conversation yesterday afternoon, pursuant to Section 83114 (d). 
As you 
recall I am in the process of purchasing a residential lot on which I intend 
to build a home. 
The substantive facts of this situation are as outlined 
below: 
I am the Director of Planning for the City of Gilroy having authority 
for recommendations to both the Planning Commission and City Council on 
virtually all land development proposals. 
Due to the small size of the 
Planning Staff, it would be impractical for me to remove myself from 
the review of development applications. 
I presently rent a cottage 
just outside the City limits, and I have been looking for a place in 
the City for more than a year. 
However I have been unable to find 
exactly what I was looking for, so I decided that perhaps I should have 
a home built. 
About six months ago a few lots which meet my needs were 
offered for sale in a new tract. 
I waited until all planning approvals 
were completed on this tract before making an offer, through a realtor, 
on a one acre lot last Thursday, March 17th. 
The asking price for the 
lot is $90,000; and my first offer was $71,800 with the present owner 
to carry a first mortgage of $50,000 for five years. 
I have not yet 
received a counter offer. 
On Monday, March 21st, I learned that the 
present partners who own the lot, in additon to a local consulting 
engineer, includes a family group which is extensively involved in land 
development in Gilroy. 
The Planning Department is presently processing 
three other requests 
this family for development of several hundred 
acres in various areas of the 
1. 
Should I still pursue 
of this lot; and if so what should I 
do to avoid any possible conflict of interes ? 
this 
? 
home 


Letter to Mr. Leidigh 
-2-
3/23/83 
If you need any additional information on this request please contact me 
at (408) 842-2137. 
MD:lg 
Sincerely, 
Michael Dorn 
Director of Planning

Should you still pursue purchase of this loti and, if 
so, what should you do to avoid any possible conflict 
of interest? 
2. 
Due to the small community, you have professional 
dealings with virtually every local architect and 
contractor. 
What additional problems could this 
situation pose for you in having your proposed home 
designed and built?
sections.conclusion: To avoid any possibility of a conflict of interest arising out of your purchase of the property, you should secure independent financing from a commercial lending institution on terms available to the general public or from relatives or other sources outside your jurisdiction (Gilroy) and thereby pay cash to the owners of the lot. Generally, a contractor or an architect will receive income from you and not become a source of income to you. Consequently, your private relationships with these individuals would not give rise to a need for disqualification in your professional relationships with them. Obviously, you will have to disqualify yourself from any role in Planning Department or City decisions affecting your own property.

sections.question_synthetic: Can the Director of Planning for the City of Gilroy purchase a residential lot from owners who have pending development applications before the Planning Department, and if so, what steps must be taken to avoid conflicts of interest, particularly given his professional relationships with local architects and contractors?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The requestor should obtain independent financing from a commercial lender or external sources to pay cash for the property, thereby avoiding a conflict of interest that would arise from accepting a $50,000 seller-financed loan from parties with pending applications. Private relationships with architects and contractors generally do not require disqualification, but he must recuse himself from decisions affecting his own property.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request letter (dated March 23, 1983) and the FPPC response letter (dated April 13, 1983). The response contains explicit QUESTIONS and ANALYSIS sections with clear conclusion and reasoning regarding Political Reform Act compliance. Statutory citations and regulatory references provided.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. 
Should you still pursue purchase of this loti and, if 
so, what should you do to avoid any possible conflict 
of interest? 
2. 
Due to the small community, you have professional 
dealings with virtually every local architect and 
contractor. 
What additional problems could this 
situation pose for you in having your proposed home 
designed and built?

CONCLUSION: To avoid any possibility of a conflict of interest arising out of your purchase of the property, you should secure independent financing from a commercial lending institution on terms available to the general public or from relatives or other sources outside your jurisdiction (Gilroy) and thereby pay cash to the owners of the lot. Generally, a contractor or an architect will receive income from you and not become a source of income to you. Consequently, your private relationships with these individuals would not give rise to a need for disqualification in your professional relationships with them. Obviously, you will have to disqualify yourself from any role in Planning Department or City decisions affecting your own property.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises Michael Dorn, Director of Planning for Gilroy, that his proposed purchase of a residential lot from owners with three pending development applications before the Planning Department would create a conflict of interest if financed through a $50,000 seller-carried mortgage, and recommends he obtain independent financing instead. The FPPC also clarifies that his professional dealings with local architects and contractors do not require disqualification unless they provide discounts based on his official status.
